worth_it_explanation: Enhanced conversions recover conversions that cookie-based tracking drops, and the recovered data feeds Smart Bidding, so the gap shows up twice, once in reporting and once in how the account bids. The check is quick. The conversion action's diagnostics in Google Ads show whether the feature is on and whether first-party data is coming through. The fix is a configuration change in the tag plus accepting the customer data terms, not a rebuild, and it improves the count from the day it is live.
intro: Enhanced conversions for web improves the accuracy of the conversions Google Ads counts by sending hashed first-party data, such as a customer's email address, with each conversion the tag records. It addresses the conversions that cookie-based measurement misses as browsers restrict third-party cookies and shorten first-party cookie lifetimes. These checks confirm that the feature is enabled, that the tag actually passes accepted first-party fields, and that the account has accepted the terms the feature depends on.

audit_checks.title: Enhanced conversions for web is not enabled on primary conversion actions
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Conversions that the cookie-based tag misses go uncounted, and Smart Bidding optimizes toward the undercount. The reported conversion volume and the bidding both run lower than the real performance, which can hold back spend on campaigns that are actually converting.

educate.base:
Enhanced conversions for web is a Google Ads feature that sends hashed first-party customer data, such as an email address a user entered on the page, alongside the conversion the tag already records. Google matches that hashed data against signed-in Google accounts to count conversions that the cookie-based tag alone would miss, for example when a browser blocks third-party cookies or a user clears them between the click and the purchase.

Without enhanced conversions, the conversion count Google Ads reports leans entirely on the cookie the conversion tag sets. Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP) shortens or removes that cookie, and a share of conversions go uncounted. Smart Bidding then optimizes toward an undercount, so the bidding works from numbers that are lower than the real ones.

Turning the feature on has two parts. The conversion action has to send the first-party data, configured through the Google tag, Google Tag Manager, or the Google Ads application programming interface (API). The account also has to accept the customer data terms in Google Ads before the feature reports matched conversions. A setup that does one without the other reads as enabled in places but never improves the count.

A few signs point to enhanced conversions being off or incomplete:
  • The conversion action shows enhanced conversions as off, or shows it on with no first-party data detected
  • The Google tag or Tag Manager configuration does not pass an email or other accepted field
  • The customer data terms were never accepted in the account
You may want to confirm both the tag configuration and the account terms before deciding the feature is working. Either one alone leaves the count unchanged.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the purchase confirmation page is where the email and order data are already present, which makes it a natural place to pass an accepted first-party field to enhanced conversions. You may want to confirm the confirmation page exposes the email to the tag at the moment the conversion fires. A value read a moment too late arrives empty and matches nothing.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the email is captured in the form rather than at a purchase, so enhanced conversions for web depends on the tag reading that field on submission. You may want to confirm the form's email reaches the conversion tag, or consider enhanced conversions for leads, which is built for this case.

learn_more.source_title: Set up enhanced conversions for web with Google Tag Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm that enhanced conversions for web is enabled on the conversion action, that the tag passes an accepted first-party field, and that the account has accepted the customer data terms.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and select a primary conversion action you want to confirm, for example a purchase or a lead.
In the conversion action's settings, find the enhanced conversions section and note whether it is on. If it is on, note whether Google reports first-party data as detected.

Open the conversion action's diagnostics and read any enhanced conversions messages. A status that reports no first-party data points to a tag that is not passing an accepted field even when the feature is switched on.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Open Tag Assistant and load a page that fires the conversion, for example a purchase confirmation page.
Find the conversion event and inspect whether it carries hashed user-provided data, such as an email field, rather than only the cookie-based identifiers.
If the conversion fires but carries no user-provided data, the feature is not receiving what it needs, regardless of the setting in the account.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per primary conversion action, whether enhanced conversions is on, whether the tag passes an accepted first-party field as seen in Tag Assistant, and whether the account accepted the customer data terms. A conversion action with the feature on, first-party data confirmed in Tag Assistant, and terms accepted passes. A conversion action with the feature off, or on but with no first-party data reaching it, is a fail recorded with the conversion action name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the enhanced conversions status and the Tag Assistant observation for each primary conversion action above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that enhanced conversions for web is not fully active on {{site_name}}'s primary conversion actions. Cookie-based measurement misses conversions when browsers restrict cookies, and without enhanced conversions those conversions go uncounted, so the reported volume and the Smart Bidding that learns from it both run lower than the real performance. {{voice_recommend}} enabling enhanced conversions on the affected conversion actions, configuring the tag to pass an accepted first-party field such as a hashed email, and accepting the customer data terms in the account. After the change, confirm in Google Tag Assistant that the conversion event carries user-provided data and watch the conversion action's diagnostics for a healthy match status.

audit_checks.title: Enhanced conversions is on but diagnostics report a degraded match status
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When enhanced conversions is on but the first-party data does not match well, the recovered conversions are fewer than the setup implies. The account looks configured, so the gap goes unnoticed, and the uplift the feature should provide is partly lost.

educate.base:
Once enhanced conversions for web is on, Google Ads reports a status for it in the conversion action's diagnostics. The status reflects whether Google is receiving accepted first-party fields, whether those fields are formatted in a way it can use, and how much of the data matches signed-in Google accounts. A feature that is switched on does not guarantee a healthy status. The data still has to arrive in the right shape.

Common reasons a status reads as degraded include a field passed unhashed where hashing is expected, an email captured after the conversion fires so it arrives empty, or a page that only sometimes exposes the field. Each of these leaves the feature reporting fewer matched conversions than the traffic would support.

The diagnostics view is the place to read this. It separates 'recording' from a healthy match and flags the specific field problems it detects. You may want to treat a degraded status as a data problem to trace in the tag rather than a setting to toggle. The switch is already on, so the issue is in the data the tag passes.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a degraded match often traces to order data that renders after the conversion tag fires, so the email reaches the tag empty on faster loads and populated on slower ones. You may want to confirm the confirmation page exposes the field before the tag runs, rather than relying on a value that arrives a moment later.

investigate.intro: Read the conversion action's enhanced conversions diagnostics and trace any degraded status back to the field the tag passes.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', select the conversion action, and open its diagnostics.
Read the enhanced conversions status and any field-level messages. Note whether Google reports the data as matching or only as recording, and which field it flags.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Load a page that fires the conversion and inspect the conversion event's user-provided data.
Check that the field is present, that it is hashed if the setup expects hashing, and that it is populated rather than empty when the tag fires.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion action with enhanced conversions on, the diagnostics status and the Tag Assistant observation of the field passed. A healthy match status with a populated, correctly formatted field passes. A degraded status traced to an unhashed, empty, or missing field is a fail recorded with the conversion action name and the field at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the diagnostics status and the field observation for each conversion action above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that enhanced conversions for web is enabled on {{site_name}} but its diagnostics report a degraded match status, so the feature recovers fewer conversions than the setup suggests. The first-party data is not reaching Google in a form it can match, which limits the uplift the feature is meant to provide. {{voice_recommend}} tracing the flagged field in the tag, confirming it is captured before the conversion fires, and formatting it as Google expects, for example hashing the email where hashing is required. After the change, recheck the conversion action's diagnostics for a healthy match status.
